vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and dashi are almost certainly fish/bonito-based, and the kitchen handles meat and fried foods throughout; assume animal cross-contact across the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, family-run neighborhood diner where some Japanese will go a long way and English is unlikely; the owner ("obachan") is described by regulars as warm and kind.

places.name: 長門館 (Nagatokan)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Toba seafood and local-cuisine restaurant with no gluten-free accommodations published. The two signature dishes are inherently wheat-heavy: tekone sushi is bonito marinated in soy sauce (standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat), and Ise udon is a wheat-flour noodle in a sweet-savory soy-based tare. Tempura (shrimp, lobster) uses wheat batter. Dashi and marinades throughout are soy-sauce based. No dedicated fryer, no allergen menu, and no gluten handling information could be found. A celiac diner should treat this as unsafe without direct confirmation; sashimi could theoretically be eaten plain but cross-contamination from shared soy sauce and a shared kitchen is unaddressed.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built almost entirely around seafood (tekone sushi with bonito, sashimi, abalone, scallops, oysters, spiny lobster, sea urchin) plus pork and beef. The base dashi and tare rely on bonito/katsuo. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are advertised. Even apparently plant-leaning items (Ise udon) are served in a fish-based, soy-sauce tare. No vegan options found.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:00-21:00 (official site); Tabelog lists 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Open seven days a week with irregular closures (不定休).
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目（鳥羽駅南口）
places.notes: Nagatokan is a long-established Toba institution founded in 1908 (Meiji 41), set in a large building a few minutes' walk from Toba Station and built to welcome travelers and tour groups arriving on the Ise-Shima coast. It draws its catch from the local fisheries, an area historic

chef_bio.background: Nagatokan is a long-established Toba institution founded in 1908 (Meiji 41), set in a large building a few minutes' walk from Toba Station and built to welcome travelers and tour groups arriving on the Ise-Shima coast. It draws its catch from the local fisheries, an area historically known for ama (diving) culture, pearl cultivation, and abundant shellfish.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house word is 'teppari', a Toba dialect expression meaning big, abundant, and luxuriously good — the spirit behind their generous portions and commitment to fresh local sea ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: Tekone sushi — bonito marinated in a sweet-savory soy sauce and folded through vinegared rice, the fisherman's dish of the Ise-Shima coast — served alongside Ise udon, the famously soft, plump wheat noodle dressed in a dark, slightly sweet tare. The kitchen is also known for generous 'teppari' seafood: abalone, scallops, oysters, sea urchin, and spiny lobster.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1908 (Meiji 41), Nagatokan has served Toba's coastal cuisine for over a century, with a building large enough to receive tour buses bringing visitors to the Ise-Shima region.
'Teppari' is local Toba dialect meaning large in form and abundant — 'luxurious and delicious' — and gives the restaurant's hearty seafood style its name.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-battered tempura (shrimp, spiny lobster) is on the menu and is fried in a shared kitchen; no dedicated fryer or allergen separation is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tekone sushi is bonito marinated in soy sauce, and Ise udon is served in a soy-based tare; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and the page gives no gluten-free soy alternative.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on seafood with bonito-based dashi and tare; even noodle dishes use a fish-based sauce, so plant-only diners cannot rely on the standard preparations.

cultural_comfort.note: A large, tour-friendly old-style seafood restaurant near the station where some basic Japanese phrases will help, as dedicated English allergy support is not indicated.

places.name: 磯揚げ まる天 鳥羽駅店 (Isoage Maruten Toba Eki-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a deep-fried fish-paste (surimi/neri-mono) takeout stand. Satsuma-age / isoage style products are made from fish paste bound with wheat starch and/or flour, so wheat is structurally present in the core product. Everything is deep-fried in shared oil with no dedicated gluten-free fryer, and the staffed kiosk setting is not equipped to handle celiac-level cross-contamination. No published allergen guarantees were available; the brand advises contacting the company directly for allergen details. Treat as not safe for celiac.
places.vegan_detail: The entire product line is built on fish paste (surimi) plus seafood and ingredients such as octopus, squid, shrimp, clam, scallop, cheese (dairy) and mayo. These are animal-based by definition, so there are no vegan options. Even the vegetable-forward pieces (ginger-lotus root, corn-cheese, potato-butter) are mixed into a fish-paste base or contain dairy.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (official site). Tabelog also lists 08:30-18:00. Open daily, no regular closing days (なし).
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅前
places.cuisine: 練り物・揚げかまぼこ
places.notes: Maruten (まる天) is a fried-fish-cake brand born in the Ise-Shima region, an area long celebrated as one of Japan's great seafood larders. The shops fry their isoage to order so customers can eat them at their freshly-made best, and the Toba Station branch sits right by the station

chef_bio.background: Maruten (まる天) is a fried-fish-cake brand born in the Ise-Shima region, an area long celebrated as one of Japan's great seafood larders. The shops fry their isoage to order so customers can eat them at their freshly-made best, and the Toba Station branch sits right by the station gates among the small specialty shops and convenience stores travelers pass on their way to the bay.
chef_bio.philosophy: Born from a wish to make a new local specialty by serving freshly fried seafood snacks in their best possible condition, using the bounty of the Ise-Shima sea.
chef_bio.specialty: Isoage — deep-fried fish-paste sticks and tempura-style pieces, including takoyaki-bo (octopus), cheese-bo, shrimp-mayo-bo, and assorted seafood-and-vegetable kakiage.

anecdotes.text: Items are fried fresh in the shop, so with good timing you can catch them just-made; a nearby waiting area lets you eat them warm on the spot.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All products are deep-fried together in shared oil; no dedicated gluten-free fryer.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core product is fish paste (surimi) plus seafood/dairy; no vegan items and animal ingredients pervade the line.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen guarantees; brand advises contacting the company directly for allergen information.

cultural_comfort.note: A grab-and-go fried fish-cake kiosk right by the station gates; quick point-and-buy with minimal conversation, fine for any comfort level but not a place to discuss allergens in depth.

places.name: 鳥羽 久兵衛 (Toba Kyubei)
places.gf_detail: A traditional yakitori izakaya with no indication of gluten-free accommodation. Yakitori is glazed in tare sauce, which is made from wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu), and the kitchen also serves fried dishes and other izakaya fare. Dashi and seasonings used for izakaya dishes typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no information on a dedicated fryer or any allergen handling. Celiacs should treat this as unsafe unless they can confirm details directly in advance, and even salt-only (shio) skewers carry shared-grill cross-contamination risk.
places.vegan_detail: This is a yakitori (grilled chicken) and izakaya specializing in Ise chicken and local seafood; the entire concept is animal-based. Even vegetable skewers and side dishes would be cooked on the same grill as chicken/fish and seasoned with bonito/katsuo dashi and wheat soy sauce. No vegan options indicated.
places.hours_raw: 18:00-24:00（営業時間・定休日は変更となる場合があるため、来店前に要確認）
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽2丁目・鳥羽駅東
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1971, Kyubei is described as the oldest yakitori izakaya in Toba, a small grill-side tavern just east of the station that has been part of the local drinking culture for decades.

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1971, Kyubei is described as the oldest yakitori izakaya in Toba, a small grill-side tavern just east of the station that has been part of the local drinking culture for decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on the local pantry: sake from Mie Prefecture, fish from local waters, and Ise chicken on the skewers.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled yakitori made with Ise chicken, served alongside local seafood and Mie-brewed sake.

anecdotes.text: Billed as the oldest yakitori izakaya in Toba, having opened in 1971.
The shop puts together a regional spread: Mie-prefecture sake, local seafood, and Ise chicken.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Yakitori is grilled on a shared charcoal grill and izakaya fare includes fried items; no allergen separation is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare and izakaya seasonings are based on standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free options are mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: An Ise-chicken yakitori and seafood izakaya with no vegan menu; vegetable items would share the grill and likely use bonito/katsuo dashi.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, long-running local yakitori izakaya with counter and tatami seating; comfortable for those at ease in a traditional Japanese izakaya setting, with little expectation of English support.

places.name: シーラウンジ S.Y.M (Sea Lounge S.Y.M)
places.gf_detail: A casual harborside kissaten (café) with no gluten-free accommodation indicated. Core menu items are inherently wheat-based or wheat-risky: butter toast in the morning set, Ise udon (wheat noodles in a soy/tamari-based broth), yakisoba, curry rice (most Japanese curry roux contains wheat flour), and fried chicken / fried sets (battered, likely sharing a common fryer). Soy sauce and dashi used in set meals typically contain wheat and bonito. No dedicated fryer, no allergen labeling, and no evidence of celiac awareness. Not suitable for a celiac without bringing your own food.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offerings are advertised. The menu is built around egg/dairy items (toast with butter and fried egg in the morning set, banana juice) and meat/fish set meals. Set meals and noodle broths almost certainly rely on katsuo (bonito) dashi and soy sauce, so even seemingly plant-based items like Ise udon or curry are not reliably vegan. A determined vegan could possibly get black coffee or plain toast (likely buttered) but would need to ask carefully; hidden dashi/katsuo is a real risk in anything savory.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽マリンターミナル2F
places.notes: Sea Lounge S.Y.M sits on the second floor of the Toba Marine Terminal, the modern ferry hub on Toba Bay that connects the mainland to the city's outlying islands. It is the kind of relaxed harborside café where island-hoppers pause before or after a boat, watching ferries and fis

chef_bio.origin: Toba, Mie Prefecture, on Toba Bay
chef_bio.background: Sea Lounge S. Y. M sits on the second floor of the Toba Marine Terminal, the modern ferry hub on Toba Bay that connects the mainland to the city's outlying islands. It is the kind of relaxed harborside café where island-hoppers pause before or after a boat, watching ferries and fishing craft slide across the water through big terminal windows.
chef_bio.philosophy: A simple, welcoming rest stop for travelers: honest café fare and homey set meals enjoyed over a view of the bay.
chef_bio.specialty: An early-morning 'morning set' (around 500 yen) of buttered toast, a fried egg, and a small salad served alongside hot or iced coffee — a generous, well-loved breakfast for the price.

anecdotes.text: Opening at 7:00 a.m., it is one of the few spots near the harbor where you can sit down to a quiet morning coffee and toast while watching the boats before catching an early ferry to the islands.
Beyond breakfast it serves comforting day-to-day plates — curry rice, Ise udon, yakisoba, and fried-chicken sets — the unfussy menu of a neighborhood terminal café.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on wheat-heavy items (toast, Ise udon, yakisoba, curry rice, fried sets); fried items likely share one fryer. No gluten-free handling indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals and Ise udon broth rely on standard Japanese soy sauce/tare, which normally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Savory dishes and noodle broths almost certainly use katsuo (bonito) dashi; the morning set includes egg and butter. No vegan options advertised.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, local ferry-terminal café where staff likely speak little English, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will help you order and ask about ingredients.

places.name: ヒノヤマラボ (Hinoyama Lab)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings or accommodations are indicated anywhere. The signature items — Belgian waffles and the muffins/baked confections — are wheat-based, and there is no published allergen breakdown or gluten-free menu. "無添加 / additive-free" refers to the absence of preservatives and additives in the puddings, NOT to gluten. The puddings (plain, espresso, Ise matcha) are likely flour-free custard but this is not confirmed and there is no celiac-safe handling, no dedicated equipment, and the kitchen is a confectionery lab actively producing wheat baked goods, so cross-contamination risk is high. A celiac should treat this as not safe unless they can confirm specific items on-site.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan food items are documented. The puddings are custard (egg/dairy) and the Belgian waffles contain dairy and egg. Vegan-friendly choices are limited to drinks: a soy latte (ソイラテ) is on the menu, along with various fruit juices (orange, apple, mango, guava, grape), tea, and beer. No plant-milk-based desserts are listed. Treat this as a drinks-only option for vegans and ask on-site about any dessert ingredients.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-17:00; closed Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday (open Thursday-Sunday)
places.neighborhood: 中之郷・扇芳閣敷地内（鳥羽駅 徒歩約9分）
places.notes: Hinoyama Lab opened in August 2023 at the foot of Mt. Hino (樋の山) on the grounds of Senpokaku, a hot-spring inn established in 1950. It was built as part of the inn's renovation, with a timber-forward structure by architect Teppei Fujiwara that blends into the surrounding forest.

roles: Operated by MUSEA, a Toba-based coffee brand
chef_bio.background: Hinoyama Lab opened in August 2023 at the foot of Mt. Hino (樋の山) on the grounds of Senpokaku, a hot-spring inn established in 1950. It was built as part of the inn's renovation, with a timber-forward structure by architect Teppei Fujiwara that blends into the surrounding forest. The cafe is run by MUSEA, a well-known local coffee brand that operates several spots around Toba including near Toba Station and the aquarium.
chef_bio.philosophy: Coffee and sweets enjoyed amid nature: a focus on coffee 'from origin through extraction' and locally sourced, preservative-free confections meant to pair with the drinks, in a quiet woodland setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Additive-free (無添加) handmade pudding — offered in plain, espresso, and Ise-matcha flavors — paired with self-roasted coffee. Belgian waffles round out the small pastry lineup.

anecdotes.text: Seating is a handful of outdoor benches tucked under an awning among the trees at the mountain's base, reached by a path past the inn's poolside — a deliberately hidden-away, laboratory-meets-forest atmosphere.
The puddings come in three flavors (plain, espresso, and Ise matcha) and are made additive-free using Mie Prefecture produce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The venue is a confectionery production lab actively making wheat-based Belgian waffles and baked goods, so shared surfaces and high gluten cross-contamination risk should be assumed. No gluten-free protocol is published.

positives.text: A soy latte is offered, giving vegans a plant-milk drink option alongside fruit juices and tea.
Puddings and confections are advertised as additive-free (無添加) using local Mie produce.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual outdoor coffee stand on a hotel's grounds; a friendly greeting and pointing at the simple menu is plenty, though staff are unlikely to handle detailed allergy questions in English.

places.name: 喫茶潮騒 (Kissa Shiosai)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee shop whose food offering is entirely wheat-based: the morning sets are an egg sandwich, ham sandwich, or toast — all served on wheat bread. No gluten-free bread, no naturally GF mains, and no dedicated fryer or GF preparation are mentioned anywhere. There is no celiac-safe option here; treat it as unsafe for a strict gluten-free diet. Drinks (coffee, tea, orange juice) are the only items without obvious gluten, but cross-contact in a small kitchen handling bread is likely and unaddressed by staff. Confirm directly if you intend to order only beverages.
places.vegan_detail: Food is not vegan-friendly: the morning sets center on egg, ham, and toast (likely with butter/margarine and dairy). No plant-based food option is documented. A vegan visitor is effectively limited to black coffee, tea, or orange juice. There is no menu indication that any savory item is free of egg, dairy, or meat, so ask before assuming anything beyond plain drinks is suitable.
places.neighborhood: 中之郷／鳥羽駅 徒歩約10分（約759m）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kissa Shiosai is the café corner on the second floor of the Toba Ferry Terminal, run in connection with the Isewan Ferry that crosses Ise Bay between Toba and the Atsumi Peninsula. It is the kind of unhurried, old-fashioned waiting-room kissaten where travelers pass the time befo

roles: cafe within Toba Ferry Terminal operated in connection with Isewan Ferry
chef_bio.background: Kissa Shiosai is the café corner on the second floor of the Toba Ferry Terminal, run in connection with the Isewan Ferry that crosses Ise Bay between Toba and the Atsumi Peninsula. It is the kind of unhurried, old-fashioned waiting-room kissaten where travelers pass the time before a sailing, looking out over the harbor.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café frames itself simply as a convenient, affordable stop for ferry passengers and Toba sightseers who have a little time to spare, with takeout available so you can carry your breakfast onto the boat.
chef_bio.specialty: Its best-known offering is the morning set, introduced in August 2021: for a flat 500 yen you get an egg sandwich, ham sandwich, or toast paired with a small salad and a choice of coffee, tea, or orange juice, served from 7:40 to 11:00 — early enough to catch before a morning ferry.

anecdotes.text: The morning service launched in August 2021 at a flat 500 yen, and the café offers takeout so passengers can eat aboard the ferry while crossing Ise Bay.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All food items are wheat-based (sandwiches and toast), so a kitchen this small almost certainly handles flour/bread throughout; no gluten-free handling is described.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg, ham, and likely butter/dairy are core to the morning sets; no separate vegan preparation is mentioned.

positives.text: Plain beverages (coffee, tea, orange juice) are available and are the safest order for restricted diets.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, no-frills ferry-terminal café where ordering is simple and staff bring food to your table; little to no English is expected, so point-to-menu or basic Japanese works best.

places.name: ミュゼア コーヒー 鳥羽水族館店 (MUSEA COFFEE Toba Aquarium)
places.gf_detail: This is a self-roast coffee stand whose food lineup is built around wheat-based bakery items — fresh-baked bread, bagels, muffins, scones, brownies, waffles, apple pie, and paninis. No gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation is advertised on the official site or listings. There is no dedicated fryer and no allergen/GF documentation found. A celiac should treat the food as not safe and confirm directly; black coffee and plain drinks are the only items likely to be free of gluten, but cross-contact in a small bakery-focused stand cannot be ruled out.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. Core items are dairy/egg-based (soft serve ice cream, baked goods, milk-based drinks like matcha frappe and tapioca milk tea). Espresso-based drinks could potentially be made with a plant milk substitution but no plant-milk option is documented, so it must be confirmed on-site. Treat baked goods as containing butter/eggs/milk unless staff confirm otherwise.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog lists 10:00–16:30, closed Tuesdays. Official site and some listings vary slightly (10:30–17:00 / 11:00–17:00); hours may track aquarium operating times, so confirm before a special trip.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽・鳥羽水族館入口横／鳥羽駅 徒歩約8分
places.notes: MUSEA is a Toba-based specialty coffee roaster that opened this branch at the entrance of Toba Aquarium in March 2016. The brand roasts beans it describes as carefully selected from around the world at its oceanside facility in Toba, building a small local group of locations that

chef_bio.background: MUSEA is a Toba-based specialty coffee roaster that opened this branch at the entrance of Toba Aquarium in March 2016. The brand roasts beans it describes as carefully selected from around the world at its oceanside facility in Toba, building a small local group of locations that includes its main general store and cafe, this aquarium stand, a branch by Kintetsu Toba Station, and the Hinoyama Lab.
chef_bio.philosophy: MUSEA frames itself around 'a special cup of coffee that feels the sea breeze,' pairing self-roasted coffee with carefully made food to enrich a guest's time by the sea.
chef_bio.specialty: House-roasted specialty coffee paired with fresh-baked bakery items and soft serve ice cream

anecdotes.text: The stand sits right at the aquarium entrance and can be used without buying an admission ticket, and drinks and snacks may be carried inside the aquarium.
MUSEA roasts its coffee at an oceanside facility in Toba and runs several local outposts, leaning on the 'sea breeze' identity of the town.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The food menu centers on wheat-based bakery items (bread, bagels, scones, muffins, brownies, waffles, apple pie, paninis), so flour is handled throughout the small stand and cross-contact is likely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soft serve, baked goods, and milk-based drinks dominate the menu; no dairy-free or egg-free items are documented.

positives.text: Cards are not accepted but electronic money and QR-code payments are, so a phone-based payment works.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual walk-up coffee stand at a major tourist aquarium, so a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the menu works fine, though detailed allergy questions in English may be hard.

places.name: クッカーニャ 鳥羽店 (Cuccagna Toba)
places.gf_detail: This is a pasta-and-pizza Italian house, so wheat is central to the core menu and there is no indication of any gluten-free pasta, pizza base, or dedicated preparation. No source mentions gluten-free options, allergy handling, or a dedicated fryer. A celiac diner should treat the kitchen as a high-cross-contamination wheat environment; if visiting, confirm directly before ordering, but expect very limited safe options.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is strongly seafood-forward (Toba turban-shell/sazae pasta, octopus pizza, oyster cream pasta, pescatore), and no source confirms a dedicated vegetarian or vegan dish. Italian kitchens like this typically offer a tomato or aglio-olio style pasta that could be adapted, but hidden animal stock, cheese, butter, or anchovy is likely and unverified. A vegan diner must ask in advance whether a plant-only pasta can be prepared without dashi/bonito, cheese, or seafood. Do not assume any item is vegan as served.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅JR口 徒歩約4分
places.notes: クッカーニャ 鳥羽店は、鳥羽駅から歩いてすぐの場所にある、おしゃれでカジュアルな雰囲気のイタリアンダイニング＆バー。1階はカウンター、2階はテーブル席という小さな二階建ての造りで、昼はお得なランチ、夜はワインとアラカルトでしっとりと大人の時間を過ごせる、地元に根ざした隠れ家的な一軒です。

roles: イタリアンダイニング
chef_bio.background: クッカーニャ 鳥羽店は、鳥羽駅から歩いてすぐの場所にある、おしゃれでカジュアルな雰囲気のイタリアンダイニング＆バー。 1階はカウンター、2階はテーブル席という小さな二階建ての造りで、昼はお得なランチ、夜はワインとアラカルトでしっとりと大人の時間を過ごせる、地元に根ざした隠れ家的な一軒です。
chef_bio.philosophy: 地元食材へのこだわりを軸に、鳥羽の海の恵みを季節感のあるイタリアンに仕立てる。
chef_bio.specialty: 地元・鳥羽で揚がる魚介を生かした料理。 サザエのパスタ、タコのピザ、牡蠣のクリームパスタ、ペスカトーレなど、港町ならではの旬の海の幸を白ワインに合わせて楽しめます。

anecdotes.text: ランチは約10種類のパスタが中心で、見栄え・味・ボリュームのバランスが評判。鳥羽の旬魚介を使った一皿が並びます。
おしゃれでカジュアルな鳥羽の隠れ家として、夜はワインとアラカルトで大人の雰囲気を楽しめると紹介されています。

cultural_comfort.note: A casual local Italian spot near the station with Japanese-language menus and staff; basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering and any dietary requests much smoother.

places.name: 太助 (Tasuke)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional neighborhood sushi counter where soy sauce (which normally contains wheat) is central to the meal, and an omakase-style flow of nigiri and sake snacks is served. No gluten-free accommodation, wheat-free soy sauce, or allergen handling is mentioned anywhere. For a celiac, the soy sauce, likely wheat-containing seasonings in simmered/marinated items (e.g., kombu-cured snapper, cooked octopus), and the shared counter prep are unmanaged risks. Treat as not safe unless you can communicate your needs directly and the chef confirms — and even then, expect uncertainty.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused Ise Bay sushi shop built around local fish, shellfish, and sake accompaniments. There is no vegan offering; the rice and most dishes are likely seasoned with fish-derived dashi/seasonings, and the menu is omakase-driven around the day's catch. Not a viable option for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: 17:00–22:00; closed Wednesday and Thursday (定休日：水・木). Opening time listed as 17:00 on the tourism association page.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽2丁目 (about a 10-minute walk from Toba Station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tasuke is a longstanding neighborhood sushi shop near Toba Station, opened roughly 46 years ago (as of 2023) and run by a husband-and-wife pair. The first-generation owner was born in 1947, and the couple have kept the place small and personal for decades, serving locals — includ

chef_bio.background: Tasuke is a longstanding neighborhood sushi shop near Toba Station, opened roughly 46 years ago (as of 2023) and run by a husband-and-wife pair. The first-generation owner was born in 1947, and the couple have kept the place small and personal for decades, serving locals — including the area's fishermen, who are regulars.
chef_bio.philosophy: A small two-person shop that aims to send travelers home with a warm memory of their Toba trip, centering everything on local Ise Bay seafood and unhurried, friendly hospitality at the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Omakase-style sushi and sake snacks built entirely around the day's catch from Ise Bay — an ever-changing lineup that has included Ise spiny lobster, black abalone, flatfish, squid, horse mackerel, ark shell, kombu-cured red snapper, octopus, and mackerel, served over traditional, slightly sweet white sushi rice.

anecdotes.text: The owner and his wife serve guests while trading jokes, keeping the mood relaxed and personal; he once quipped that he wouldn't hand the shop down to his son over concerns about appearances.
Local fishermen are among the regulars, and a visitor described the run of Ise Bay fish here as 'an Ise Bay paradise.'
The shop describes itself as a small place where a couple serve customers with their Toba trip memories in mind.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi counter, soy sauce is integral to the meal and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no wheat-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop is centered entirely on Ise Bay fish and shellfish with sake accompaniments; no plant-based or vegan items are offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny counter run by an elderly couple who chat warmly with guests, but it's a Japanese-language local sushi shop with no signs of English support or allergy handling, so some Japanese (or a translation tool and patience) will help a lot.

places.name: 伊勢志摩かりんとー (Iseshima Karinto)
places.gf_detail: This is a karintō (deep-fried sweet) specialty shop whose core product is dough made from Mie-grown wheat flour (Ayahikari variety) deep-fried in rice oil. Wheat is the primary, unavoidable ingredient in essentially every product. There are no gluten-free options and this is not a safe stop for celiac diners. The shop is takeout-only with no kitchen accommodations.
places.vegan_detail: Products are wheat-dough karintō fried in rice oil, with many plant-based flavors (Ise tea, sesame, brown sugar, aonori seaweed, mandarin, okara). However at least one signature flavor is 煮干し/いりこ (dried sardine/anchovy salt), which is not vegan, and other flavorings are not confirmed. Because multiple flavors share frying and production, a vegan should ask which specific flavors are free of fish/animal ingredients before buying; some flavors appear plant-based but this is not verified per-item.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目（鳥羽一番街・ミキモト真珠島そば）
places.cuisine: 和菓子・かりんとう専門
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 伊勢志摩かりんとー was launched in August 2016 by みえづくし合同会社 (Mie-Dzukushi LLC), a company formed earlier that year by three local Toba business owners who jokingly called themselves the 'おじさん' (the uncles). Watching younger entrepreneurs revitalize empty storefronts, the trio decided to s

roles: 吉水理裕 (Masahiro Yoshimizu) — co-founder, runs Edion Yoshimizu electronics store
岩佐政徳 (Masanori Iwasa) — co-founder, runs Iwasa Construction
森下功一 (Koichi Morishita) — co-founder, runs the Morishita sake shop
chef_bio.background: 伊勢志摩かりんとー was launched in August 2016 by みえづくし合同会社 (Mie-Dzukushi LLC), a company formed earlier that year by three local Toba business owners who jokingly called themselves the 'おじさん' (the uncles). Watching younger entrepreneurs revitalize empty storefronts, the trio decided to start something of their own to champion Mie's regional ingredients.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing Mie Prefecture's local produce — Mie wheat, Tamaki-cho okara, regional rice oil and seaweed — in a familiar nostalgic snack, with minimal use of chemical seasonings, as a form of local revitalization.
chef_bio.specialty: Karintō (traditional deep-fried sweets) made from Mie-grown Ayahikari wheat flour and okara, fried in locally produced rice oil, sold in a range of regional flavors such as Ise tea, sesame, brown sugar, aonori seaweed, and Yamato-tachibana.

anecdotes.text: The three founders were spurred to act when they felt the local young entrepreneurs were silently asking, 'So what are you uncles going to start?' — and decided 'やったろうやないか!' (let's give it a go!).
The karintō are made with Mie's Ayahikari wheat, okara from Tamaki-cho soybeans, and rice oil from Kuwana — said to be the birthplace of rice oil in Japan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire product line is wheat-based karintō; wheat flour is the main ingredient of the dough, so there is no gluten-free option and contamination is inherent.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Flavors include a dried-sardine/anchovy (煮干し/いりこ) salt variety, which is not vegan; multiple flavors share the same frying and production, so cross-contact between fish and plant-based flavors is possible.

positives.text: Ingredients are simple and locally sourced (Mie wheat, Tamaki-cho okara, rice oil, regional seaweed/sesame/tea) with minimal chemical seasonings, making the ingredient list relatively transparent.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local souvenir shop where staff likely speak mainly Japanese; pointing at flavors works fine for a quick takeout purchase, but expect little English.

places.name: 泉門天 (Senmonten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published and no accommodation is indicated. As a yakiniku shop, the dipping tare and any soy sauce almost certainly contain wheat, and a single shared grill plus a 6-seat kitchen means high cross-contact risk. Plain salt-grilled cuts (e.g., tan-shio) could in theory be lower-risk, but this is unconfirmed and would require a careful conversation in Japanese; treat as not safe for celiacs without direct verification.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused yakiniku and hormone (offal) specialist built around Matsusaka beef, beef tongue, yukhoe (raw beef), and grilled offal. There is no vegan menu, and even side items like kimchi and the tare/dipping sauces would carry animal- and bonito/dashi-derived risks. Not a viable choice for vegan diners.
places.hours_raw: Tuesday–Sunday 18:00–21:00 (one source lists evening service until 22:00); closed Mondays. Dinner only.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽・中之郷
places.notes: Senmonten is a small, traditional yakiniku and hormone (offal) shop tucked into the Toba waterfront, a short walk from both Nakanosato and Toba stations. It is the kind of intimate, owner-run counter where the master (taisho) greets regulars and visitors alike with bright, easy h

chef_bio.background: Senmonten is a small, traditional yakiniku and hormone (offal) shop tucked into the Toba waterfront, a short walk from both Nakanosato and Toba stations. It is the kind of intimate, owner-run counter where the master (taisho) greets regulars and visitors alike with bright, easy hospitality.
chef_bio.philosophy: Serve top-grade local beef simply and generously in a calm, homey, Japanese-style room where families and children are warmly welcomed.
chef_bio.specialty: Premium Matsusaka beef grilled over the table — the special loin (特選ロース) and kalbi (short rib) are the signature cuts, described as melting in the mouth, alongside beef tongue (牛タン), yukhoe, and offal such as tecchan.

anecdotes.text: The interior is calm and traditional Japanese in style, and the proprietor's bright hospitality is part of the draw; families with children are very welcome.
Beyond the beef, the shop is proud of award-winning rice and house kimchi served as sides.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Single small yakiniku shop (around 6 seats) with table grilling, implying shared grills and surfaces and high cross-contact risk; no GF protocol is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakiniku service relies on tare dipping sauce and soy sauce, which in Japan typically contain wheat; no gluten-free sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on beef, beef tongue, yukhoe and offal; sides include kimchi and rice, with no plant-based or dashi-free options indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, traditional, owner-run yakiniku counter where the proprietor chats warmly with guests; basic Japanese or a translation app will help, and there is no sign of English service.

places.name: 御食事処 花さんご (Oshokujidokoro Hanasango)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. This is a casual aquarium food court-style eatery whose signature dishes are Japanese-style curry rice, loco moco, and roast beef plates. Japanese curry is made from a wheat-flour roux and is not safe for celiacs; loco moco gravy and any teriyaki/demi-glace sauces also typically contain wheat. No allergen menu, ingredient sourcing, or cross-contamination information is published. A dedicated celiac should treat this as unsafe unless they can confirm specifics on-site (and language/staff capacity to do so is unverified).
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering confirmed. The menu centers on meat dishes (roast beef, loco moco) and curry; the curry is described as appealing to children but there is no statement that it is meat-free or animal-product-free, and Japanese curry roux commonly contains animal fats/dashi. The broccoli/potato garnish on the otter-shaped "Mei-chan's Curry" is decorative, not a standalone vegan plate. No source documents a vegetable-only or plant-based option. The earlier "veggie-leaning dish" framing is not supported by any page reviewed.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽・中之郷（鳥羽水族館内）
places.cuisine: 郷土料理・洋食・カレー
places.notes: Hanasango is the in-house restaurant of Toba Aquarium in Mie Prefecture, where guests dine alongside a large coral-reef tank so fish drift past the windows during the meal.

chef_bio.background: Hanasango is the in-house restaurant of Toba Aquarium in Mie Prefecture, where guests dine alongside a large coral-reef tank so fish drift past the windows during the meal.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans into playful, photogenic, family-friendly plates designed from a 'women's-eye' perspective, alongside hearty options like loco moco and roast beef.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is the otter-shaped 'Mei-chan's Curry,' modeled on Mei, the aquarium's beloved sea otter, served with broccoli and star-cut potato; it is offered at lunchtime only, while the Walrus Parfait is served outside lunch hours.

anecdotes.text: The signature curry is shaped after the aquarium's idol sea otter 'Mei,' and is offered only during lunchtime, while the Walrus Parfait is sold during non-lunch hours.
Diners eat while watching marine life in a large reef tank, giving families a relaxed break inside the aquarium.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, family-oriented restaurant inside Toba Aquarium with no published English support or allergen handling, so a GF/vegan diner should not rely on it for safe dietary accommodation and is best served by guidance before visiting.

places.name: 四季のご馳走家 味彩 (Shiki no Gochisouya Ajisai)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional seafood izakaya with no stated gluten-free accommodation. Core preparations rely on wheat-containing Japanese seasonings: sashimi and shellfish are served with regular soy sauce (typically brewed with wheat), and signature dishes such as wakame with bonito-dashi ponzu use soy-based ponzu. Fried items (e.g. fried oysters) and tempura are on the menu and would share a fryer with breaded/wheat-floured items — no dedicated fryer is mentioned. Even the fresh sashimi (the safest-looking option) is normally eaten with shoyu. A celiac diner should not assume safety here; at minimum they would need to bring their own GF tamari, confirm raw fish is plated separately, and avoid anything fried or sauced. No cross-contamination controls are documented.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-centric izakaya whose identity is fresh fish and shellfish from local ports and from the owner's ama-diver mother — there is no vegan menu or stated plant-based focus. Japanese izakaya cooking here leans on animal-derived umami: bonito (katsuo) dashi is explicitly used (e.g. wakame with bonito-dashi ponzu), and most simmered/dressed vegetable dishes in this style hide katsuo or fish stock. A vegan would have very little to safely order and must ask in detail about every dish; assume hidden dashi/bonito throughout. Not a destination for vegan dining.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:45–14:00 and 17:00–24:00 (dinner L.O. ~22:45); closed Sunday. Note: the official site lists dinner-only hours of 17:00–23:00 daily, so hours may vary — confirm before visiting.
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born in Toba City in October 1970, the owner-chef spent 18 years working in hotels and izakaya kitchens before opening his own place specializing in the freshly caught seafood of his hometown.

chef_bio.background: Born in Toba City in October 1970, the owner-chef spent 18 years working in hotels and izakaya kitchens before opening his own place specializing in the freshly caught seafood of his hometown.
chef_bio.philosophy: To serve guests seafood at its freshest — straight from the local fishing port and the diving grounds.
chef_bio.specialty: A fresh sashimi platter built from the day's catch and from live fish guests can pick straight out of the in-store tank, alongside shellfish brought in by the chef's mother.

anecdotes.text: The chef's mother is still an active ama (free-diving fisherwoman) at 72, and she supplies the shellfish for the restaurant.
As a boy he would visit his mother's seaside ama hut and eat the seafood dishes the divers cooked there — an experience he credits with setting him on his culinary path.
Diners can choose live fish from the tank in the shop to be prepared as sashimi to order.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried oysters and tempura, which in an izakaya kitchen share fryer oil with wheat-breaded items; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and shellfish are served with standard soy sauce, and a signature wakame dish uses bonito-dashi ponzu (soy-based) — typical Japanese shoyu/ponzu is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito (katsuo) dashi is explicitly used (e.g. wakame with bonito-dashi ponzu); fish stock is likely present in many dishes that otherwise appear vegetable-based.

positives.text: Very fresh raw fish and shellfish straight from the tank and local port means plain sashimi can be eaten without sauce if the diner brings or requests a wheat-free alternative.

cultural_comfort.note: A local Toba seafood izakaya run by an owner-chef and his ama-diver family; warm and welcoming to both residents and visitors, but expect a Japanese-only environment, so basic Japanese or a translation aid helps for ordering and explaining dietary needs.

places.name: 和食堂 いせえび (Washokudo Iseebi)
places.gf_detail: This is a casual Japanese seafood eatery with no gluten-free program. Signature dishes are gluten-heavy: jumbo shrimp fry / ebi-fry curry (wheat-flour breading, shared fryer with other fried items), and set meals served with miso soup and Japanese sides. Sashimi and grilled dishes are served with standard soy sauce (contains wheat) and Japanese dashi commonly used in simmered/seasoned dishes. No dedicated fryer, no gluten-free soy sauce, and no allergen handling protocol is documented. A celiac should treat this place as not safe without bringing tamari and confirming preparation in person.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around seafood — live Ise lobster, abalone, sashimi, fisherman's rice bowl, and shrimp dishes. Even rice/vegetable items would typically rely on katsuo/bonito dashi and the kitchen's standard fish-based seasonings. No vegan options are advertised and the concept is fundamentally fish-centric, so a vegan diner cannot be reliably accommodated.
places.hours_raw: 9:00～17:30（冬季平日・日曜は～17:00まで）／定休日：水曜（祝日と8月1～15日は営業）
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 和食堂 いせえび sits inside TOBA Pearl Town along the road to the Toba Aquarium, in one of the few spots in Toba where you can eat while looking out over Toba Bay toward Mikimoto Pearl Island and the boats crossing the water.

chef_bio.background: 和食堂 いせえび sits inside TOBA Pearl Town along the road to the Toba Aquarium, in one of the few spots in Toba where you can eat while looking out over Toba Bay toward Mikimoto Pearl Island and the boats crossing the water.
chef_bio.philosophy: A relaxed, family-friendly place to enjoy the region's signature catch with a sea view rather than a formal fine-dining experience.
chef_bio.specialty: Local Ise-Shima seafood — live Ise lobster (伊勢海老) and abalone (served live or butter-grilled), the fisherman's rice bowl (漁師丼), shrimp-fry curry, and sashimi set meals.

anecdotes.text: It is described as one of the very few restaurants in Toba where you can dine while gazing over the bay at Pearl Island and passing ships.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature fried items (jumbo shrimp fry, ebi-fry curry) use wheat-flour breading and would share fryer oil with other fried foods; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and set meals are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free/tamari alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely seafood-focused (lobster, abalone, fish, shrimp) and seasoned with fish-based dashi, so no vegan-safe separation exists.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual tourist-area seafood diner with little to no English support or allergen handling, so a GF/vegan visitor should rely on a guide or detailed written Japanese cards rather than expecting accommodation.

places.name: 本家かまどや 鳥羽店 (Honke Kamadoya Toba-ten)
places.gf_detail: A branch of a large takeout-bento chain. The base is white rice (国産米), and many items let you swap or choose components, so a plain-rice-plus-careful-side meal is conceivable — but this is NOT a celiac-safe environment without checking. The chain's own allergen notice lists 小麦 (wheat) among the 8 mandatory allergens and warns that tempura items share a common batter (共通の天ぷら衣) and that factory/store production, cooking and plating are shared, so trace cross-contact with wheat is possible. Soy sauce (used in many sides and the standard seasoning) is wheat-brewed; the chain cites the common position that wheat allergen is broken down during soy-sauce brewing, but that does not make a dish certified gluten-free, and there is no dedicated fryer or GF preparation. A celiac diner must consult the chain's per-item allergen search (アレルゲン検索) and confirm with staff before ordering; treat as ask, not safe.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling. As a mainstream Japanese bento chain, the offerings center on fried chicken, fish, pork and egg-based dishes; sauces and simmered sides commonly contain katsuo/bonito dashi, and the shared cooking environment means cross-contact with animal ingredients. A plant-only meal is not offered as such — at best a diner could ask for plain rice plus a vegetable side and verify ingredients, so vegan is ask/effectively limited. Verify dashi and seasoning with staff.
places.hours_raw: 公式サイト: 8:00～18:00（13:00～16:00 準備中）, 定休日 毎週日曜日。Tabelog: 平日(月～金) 8:30～19:00 / 土日祝 8:30～18:00, 不定休。営業時間は情報源により差異あり。
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽（鳥羽駅から約319m）
places.notes: 本家かまどや (Honke Kamadoya) is one of western Japan's long-running made-to-order bento chains, known for hot boxed lunches prepared at the shop and built around carefully chosen domestic rice (国産米). The Toba branch is a small local outpost a few minutes from Toba Station, serving com

chef_bio.origin: Western Japan bento chain (本家かまどや)
chef_bio.background: 本家かまどや (Honke Kamadoya) is one of western Japan's long-running made-to-order bento chains, known for hot boxed lunches prepared at the shop and built around carefully chosen domestic rice (国産米). The Toba branch is a small local outpost a few minutes from Toba Station, serving commuters, workers and families who want an affordable hot meal to take away — and it also runs a local delivery service.
chef_bio.philosophy: Affordable, freshly assembled boxed lunches using selected domestic rice, with a menu built so customers can choose and customize their box.
chef_bio.specialty: Hot, made-to-order bento boxed lunches over white rice, with the option to swap the rice base for takikomi gohan (+¥110) or fried rice (+¥170).

anecdotes.text: The shop offers local delivery (配達) and notes it will even take orders on its regular closing days by prior arrangement (休業日の予約承ります／要相談).
The chain emphasizes using carefully selected domestic rice: 本家かまどやでは厳選した国産米を使用しております.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The chain's allergen notice states that factory and store production, cooking and plating are shared across products, so an item may pick up trace amounts of allergens (including wheat) not in its own recipe.
Tempura items in some bento are all cooked in a common batter: 一部のお弁当に使用している天ぷらは、全ての食材を共通の天ぷら衣で調理しています。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chain notes the common view that wheat allergen is broken down during soy-sauce brewing, so soy sauce need not necessarily be removed for wheat allergy — but this does not make a dish certified gluten-free for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: のり, ちりめんじゃこ and あおさ used in some bento are harvested by methods that also catch shrimp and crab (えび・かに が混ざる漁法), indicating shellfish/animal cross-contact in seemingly plant items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chain publishes a per-item allergen search and an 8-allergen disclosure (小麦・そば・卵・乳・落花生・えび・かに・くるみ) and advises confirming current information, as allergen/nutrition data is updated periodically.

positives.text: Base is white rice using selected domestic rice, and the menu allows swapping the rice and choosing components, giving a careful diner room to build a simpler box.
A published online allergen lookup tool exists, so ingredients can be checked per item before ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: A neighborhood branch of a national takeout-bento chain; ordering is simple counter Japanese and English is not expected, so come ready to point at the menu or use a few Japanese phrases.

places.name: 弁慶 (Benkei)
places.gf_detail: A traditional neighborhood Chinese diner (machi-chuka). No gluten-free accommodation is advertised or known. Expect wheat throughout: ramen and fried-rice/stir-fry seasonings use wheat-based soy sauce, gyoza and noodle dishes are wheat, and dishes like sweet-and-sour pork and mapo are typically thickened/seasoned with soy-based sauces. There is no indication of a dedicated fryer or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs; nothing here can be confirmed gluten-free.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling exists. As a meat-and-broth Chinese diner, most dishes contain pork, egg, and animal-based stock; ramen broth and stir-fry sauces likely use chicken/pork base, and katsuo/animal dashi may appear in seasonings. A vegetable stir-fry could in theory be made without meat, but this is unconfirmed and would require asking in person — assume animal ingredients unless staff confirm otherwise.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽2丁目・鳥羽駅周辺
places.cuisine: 昭和レトロな町中華の個人店。酢豚・麻婆豆腐・中華飯・ラーメン・チャーハンなど、地元に愛さ
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-standing, family-run Chinese diner (machi-chuka) in Toba's 2-chome neighborhood, a short walk from Toba Station. It carries the nostalgic Showa-era atmosphere of a classic local eatery, the kind of place regulars return to for an everyday meal.

chef_bio.background: A long-standing, family-run Chinese diner (machi-chuka) in Toba's 2-chome neighborhood, a short walk from Toba Station. It carries the nostalgic Showa-era atmosphere of a classic local eatery, the kind of place regulars return to for an everyday meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Generous, affordable Chinese set meals — sweet-and-sour pork (酢豚) and other teishoku, alongside ramen, fried rice, mapo tofu and chive stir-fry.

cultural_comfort.note: A local neighborhood Chinese diner where Japanese is expected and English is unlikely; basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smoother.

places.name: 居酒屋味のやまもと (Izakaya Aji no Yamamoto)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is advertised. This is a traditional izakaya where wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) and bonito/kombu dashi are pervasive, including on sashimi (served with shoyu) and in simmered/seasoned dishes. Tempura (shrimp, chikuwa) is on the menu, so the kitchen handles wheat batter and there is no indication of a dedicated fryer. A celiac would need to confirm directly with the owner; raw sashimi could potentially be eaten plain with a self-supplied tamari, but no GF claims are made and cross-contamination risk is unconfirmed.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-focused izakaya with no vegan menu. Even non-meat dishes in this style of restaurant typically rely on bonito (katsuo) and kombu dashi, and shoyu/mirin seasonings. Hidden fish stock should be assumed throughout. A vegan would need to ask the owner directly about any plant-only options; none are documented.
places.hours_raw: 月・水〜日・祝・祝前日 17:00〜翌0:00（L.O. 24:00）。火曜定休。（食べログでは土日 11:00〜翌0:00 との記載もあり要確認）
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅
places.cuisine: 個人経営の居酒屋。市場仕入れの地魚刺身
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, owner-run izakaya a short walk from Toba Station, in the heart of a town famous for its working fishing port and pearl islands. The proprietor buys seasonal local seafood at the market and builds the menu around what comes in fresh that day.

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run izakaya a short walk from Toba Station, in the heart of a town famous for its working fishing port and pearl islands. The proprietor buys seasonal local seafood at the market and builds the menu around what comes in fresh that day.
chef_bio.philosophy: Centered on fresh, market-sourced local fish and a warm, neighborhood welcome.
chef_bio.specialty: Day-fresh local sashimi, including bonito (katsuo), alongside Toba seafood and home-style izakaya cooking.

anecdotes.text: The owner runs a personal pick-up and drop-off service for guests within roughly a 4-5km radius of the shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura (shrimp and chikuwa) is on the menu, so wheat batter is fried in the kitchen; no dedicated gluten-free fryer is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional izakaya, sashimi and dishes are served with standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood-focused kitchen where bonito/kombu dashi and fish are used throughout; assume hidden fish stock in seasoned dishes.

positives.text: Sashimi and seafood are sourced fresh from the local market, and the owner-operator is hands-on, which can make direct dietary conversation easier.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locally-run izakaya with counter and tatami seating where some Japanese and a willingness to communicate with the owner will go a long way, especially for any dietary requests.

places.name: お食事処 とまゑ (Oshokujidokoro Tomae)
places.gf_detail: This is a late-night noodle counter serving ramen, udon and soba — all wheat-based — plus assorted snacks; broths and seasonings rely on wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or handling is offered, and there is no dedicated fryer. The parent Toda-ya ryokan can accommodate wheat allergies on its main meal plans (advance request via its allergy form) but explicitly warns of shared-kitchen cross-contamination, and that program does not extend to this night counter. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on ramen, udon and soba whose broths typically use katsuo (bonito) dashi, with snacks and beer alongside — no vegan items are listed. The Toda-ya ryokan can arrange vegetarian meals (with or without egg/dairy) on its main meal plans by advance request, but that is not strict vegan and does not apply to this night-dining counter. Strict vegans should not rely on this spot.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅至近（戸田家 嬉春亭2階）
places.notes: とまゑは、伊勢志摩・鳥羽駅すぐの老舗大型旅館「戸田家」の館内にある夜食処です。お風呂あがりや宴のあとに「もう少し」と立ち寄れる場として、嬉春亭2階に設けられています。

chef_bio.background: とまゑは、伊勢志摩・鳥羽駅すぐの老舗大型旅館「戸田家」の館内にある夜食処です。 お風呂あがりや宴のあとに「もう少し」と立ち寄れる場として、嬉春亭2階に設けられています。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「粋な味処」を掲げ、旅の夜にひと息つける時間を提供することを身上とする。
chef_bio.specialty: 湯あがりに啜る一杯のラーメン。 うどん・そばや各種おつまみ、ビールも揃う、宿の夜をしめくくる気軽な麺と肴の場。

anecdotes.text: 戸田家は宿の夜食処とまゑを18時から24時まで営業し、19時～23時にはルームサービスにも対応している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Parent ryokan states meals are cooked in the same kitchen using shared cooking and washing equipment, so allergens (including wheat) may be introduced during preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu is built on ramen, udon and soba with broths and seasonings that rely on wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free option is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodle broths typically use bonito (katsuo) dashi and the counter serves meat/fish snacks; no vegan separation is described.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The Toda-ya ryokan can accommodate allergies (egg, dairy, peanut, buckwheat, wheat, soy) and vegetarian meals on its main meal plans by advance request via its allergy form, but advises severe-allergy guests to consult in advance and may decline; this is not stated to cover the Tomae night counter.

positives.text: The ryokan labels the 7 major allergens at its buffet venue, showing some allergen-awareness in the broader operation.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an in-house night-dining counter of the Toda-ya ryokan, geared toward Japanese-speaking hotel guests after the bath or an evening gathering, so expect a Japanese-only setting.

places.name: かっぱ寿司 鳥羽店 (Kappa Sushi Toba)
places.gf_detail: Conveyor-belt sushi: plain nigiri & sashimi (rice + fish) are naturally wheat-free, BUT house soy sauce contains wheat (bring your own GF tamari) and the shared belt/kitchen handles tempura, fried items & imitation crab — cross-contamination is a real risk. No GF program; assemble from plain nigiri/sashimi with care and confirm with staff.
places.vegan_detail: Plant-based picks: kappa-maki (cucumber roll), inari (sweet tofu pouch), corn gunkan, natto-maki, edamame, plain rice. Many items use fish/egg/dashi, so stick to those — but a light vegan meal is doable here.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目・鳥羽駅 徒歩圏
places.notes: [Lifeline] À-la-carte conveyor sushi near Toba Station — order plate by plate, the most flexible GF/vegan fallback in town.

gf_cross_contamination.text: 公式アレルゲン情報に「各食材の製造工場や店舗での調理過程において、他の製品と製造ラインや厨房設備を共有している等の理由により、アレルギー原材料が付着・混入する場合がございます」と明記。回転レーンや共有厨房での小麦の交差接触リスクがある。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 卓上の醤油は原材料に小麦を含む。醸造過程で小麦タンパクは大きく分解されるが、セリアックには完全な安全保証とはならない。卓上調味料（醤油・タバスコ等）はアレルギー情報の対象外と明記されている。

vegan_cross_contact.text: シャリの酢飯や各種だし・つゆに鰹など動物性が含まれる可能性があり、公開情報では確認できないため店舗確認が必要。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 公式は8品目＋準ずる品目のアレルゲン表示PDFを提供し「原材料や製造工程は変更されることがあるため来店直前の確認が必須」としている。具体的な店頭での個別対応プロトコルは公開されていない。

positives.text: わさび抜き・醤油なしで寿司ネタ単品や刺身を選べば、小麦含有調味料を避けやすい。アレルゲン表示PDFを公開しており事前確認が可能。

cultural_comfort.note: 大手回転寿司チェーンでタッチパネル注文が中心のため英語メニューや操作で言葉に頼らず注文しやすいが、アレルギー相談はスタッフへの口頭確認が必要で日本語があると安心です。

places.name: 大衆居酒屋 みしお (Taishu Izakaya Mishio)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a casual seafood izakaya where standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat), bonito/kombu dashi, and battered/fried small plates are core to the menu. There is no published allergy information, no indication of wheat-free soy sauce, and no dedicated fryer. A celiac diner should treat this as high cross-contamination risk and confirm everything directly in person; sashimi (raw fish without sauce) may be the only relatively lower-risk option, but soy sauce for dipping would still contain wheat.
places.vegan_detail: A local-fish and seafood izakaya; the menu is built around tuna, sea bream, amberjack, grilled scallops and hot pots. Even seemingly plant-based items (edamame, simmered dishes, nabe broths) are very likely seasoned with bonito (katsuo) or fish-based dashi. No vegan menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for vegans without extensive in-person confirmation, and even then options would be minimal.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目（鳥羽駅から徒歩約6分）
places.cuisine: 大衆居酒屋・地魚／海鮮
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A casual, hidden-gem style izakaya near Toba Station that opened in mid-2019, serving the local fishing town's catch in a relaxed, neighborly setting with private-room seating.

chef_bio.origin: Toba, Mie — a working port town on Ise-Shima Bay known for its fresh seafood.
chef_bio.background: A casual, hidden-gem style izakaya near Toba Station that opened in mid-2019, serving the local fishing town's catch in a relaxed, neighborly setting with private-room seating.
chef_bio.philosophy: A friendly opening message — roughly 'we've just opened, thank you for your support, we'll do our best' — captures its earnest, community-minded spirit.
chef_bio.specialty: Local fish and seafood: tuna belly (toro), seared sea bream, amberjack (kanpachi), grilled scallops, and seasonal hot pots, paired with a broad selection of Japanese sake, shochu, wine and regional Mie drinks.

anecdotes.text: Opened in June 2019 with a heartfelt 'just opened, thank you, we'll do our best' greeting to the neighborhood.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional seafood izakaya, sashimi and grilled dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on fish and shellfish, and broths/simmered dishes are very likely made with bonito (katsuo) or fish dashi; assume animal-derived stock throughout.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighborhood izakaya with no published English support, so a few basic Japanese phrases (and patience explaining dietary needs) will go a long way here.

places.name: 久兵衛 (Kyubei (Toba Kyubei))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program and no information published about wheat or allergen handling. As a traditional yakitori/izakaya, the core risks are: yakitori tare and most seasonings made with wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu); dashi and broths likely seasoned with soy sauce/mirin; and fried items (the menu includes fried shrimp, crispy potato-bacon, and seared/fried dishes) sharing oil with breaded items — no dedicated fryer is indicated. Shio (salt-only) skewers may exist but cross-contact with tare brushes and shared grill is unconfirmed. A celiac should not assume any dish is safe without a detailed phone conversation in Japanese; treat as ask-only, leaning toward no.
places.vegan_detail: A long-running yakitori and seafood izakaya with no advertised vegan or vegetarian menu. Nearly all dishes are built around chicken, pork, or fish; even the complimentary appetizer is local wakame seaweed and the tsukidashi/small plates lean on fish-based dashi and bonito. Tare (sweet skewer glaze) and most seasonings are soy-sauce based. A vegan diner would need to call ahead and ask directly whether anything (e.g. plain grilled vegetables) can be prepared without animal-derived dashi, broth, or bonito — assume hidden katsuo/fish stock otherwise.
places.hours_raw: 18:00–24:00 (one source lists 17:00–23:30). Closed Sundays (open on Sunday when the following Monday is a public holiday; one source also notes a closure on the third Monday of the month).
places.neighborhood: Toba (near Toba Station / Toba Aquarium), Toba City, Mie
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-standing yakitori and seafood izakaya in central Toba, just a short walk from the station and the bay. It is run as a family business: the parents keep the izakaya going while their son helps in the kitchen and runs a sister sushi restaurant nearby. The son trained in Aust

roles: Family-run: parents run the izakaya; their son cooks and developed new menu items
chef_bio.origin: Toba, Mie — a port town whose menu leans on local fish and Mie-prefecture chicken and sake.
chef_bio.background: A long-standing yakitori and seafood izakaya in central Toba, just a short walk from the station and the bay. It is run as a family business: the parents keep the izakaya going while their son helps in the kitchen and runs a sister sushi restaurant nearby. The son trained in Australia before returning to develop new dishes for the family table.
chef_bio.philosophy: An unpretentious neighborhood izakaya built on Ise chicken, the day's local catch, and Mie sake, with a warm, low-key family character.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled Ise-dori (Ise chicken) skewers — a signature being the Ise-chicken skewer in spicy miso (伊勢とりのぴり辛みそ焼き) — alongside an original seared Spanish-mackerel sashimi with charred skin (さわらの炙り揚げ刺) and an unusually wide range of yakitori.

anecdotes.text: The complimentary appetizer (tsukidashi) is bright local wakame seaweed, a small nod to the surrounding Toba coast.

Beyond classic skewers the kitchen serves playful originals like 'jagabe' (crispy potato and bacon) and 'tomabe' (cherry-tomato and bacon skewers), reflecting the son's new-menu experiments after training abroad.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional yakitori and izakaya kitchen with fried items on the menu (fried shrimp, seared/fried dishes); no dedicated gluten-free fryer or allergen protocol is indicated, so shared oil and shared grill cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare (skewer glaze) and izakaya seasonings are soy-sauce based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu and complimentary dishes (e.g. wakame tsukidashi, small plates) typically rely on fish/bonito dashi; an almost entirely meat- and seafood-based menu makes hidden animal stock likely. No vegan items advertised.

positives.text: Small family-run counter where the cooks are right in front of you, making it easier to ask directly about ingredients before ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locals-oriented family izakaya with counter and tatami seating and no indication of English support, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) goes a long way, especially for explaining dietary needs.

places.name: 魚新 (Uoshin)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published for this shop. As a traditional sushi shop, the default soy sauce (shoyu) almost certainly contains wheat, and miso soup/dashi commonly do as well. Sushi rice (vinegared rice) and most raw nigiri toppings are naturally wheat-free, but tamago (egg), imitation crab, tempura, and any fried items are typical gluten risks. There is no indication of a dedicated fryer or allergen protocol. A celiac diner must call ahead, explain wheat allergy clearly (小麦アレルギー), and consider bringing their own GF tamari, since cross-contamination on shared surfaces and brushes is unaddressed.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional local sushi shop centered on fresh local fish; no vegan offerings are advertised, and sushi-counter staples (dashi, miso soup, egg, and the fish itself) typically rely on katsuo/bonito and animal ingredients. No vegan menu has been confirmed. A vegan diner would need to call ahead and discuss what, if anything, is possible.
places.hours_raw: Hours not published online.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽2丁目
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independent sushi shop tucked into the Toba 2-chome neighborhood, a short walk from Toba Station in this fishing-port town on the Ise-Shima coast. Like many shops here, it leans on the abundant local catch that gives the area its reputation for sushi and seafood.

chef_bio.origin: 鳥羽市（三重県・伊勢志摩）
chef_bio.background: A small, independent sushi shop tucked into the Toba 2-chome neighborhood, a short walk from Toba Station in this fishing-port town on the Ise-Shima coast. Like many shops here, it leans on the abundant local catch that gives the area its reputation for sushi and seafood.

cultural_comfort.note: A small independent neighborhood sushi shop with no English-facing presence; basic Japanese and a polite phone call ahead will go a long way.

places.name: メインダイニング シーホース (Main Dining Sea Horse)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or GF certification. This is classic resort French cuisine (flour-based sauces, butter roux, bread course) so default dishes are not GF. The hotel does accept food-allergy requests via a downloadable allergy sheet submitted at least 4 days before the visit, and lists wheat (小麦) and soy (大豆) among handled allergens. However, the hotel explicitly states it may DECLINE requests where even trace cross-contamination could trigger a severe reaction, or where there are multiple allergies. There is no dedicated fryer and no isolated GF prep area, so a strict celiac should treat this as ask-only and not assume safe accommodation — confirm directly before booking.
places.vegan_detail: A dedicated Vegan Course (ヴィーガンコース, around ¥12,100) is offered using French technique, and the hotel also markets a vegan-course stay plan. Because the standard kitchen relies heavily on seafood, butter, cream and likely fish/bonito-based stocks, vegans must order the dedicated vegan course rather than asking for modified standard dishes; confirm the course covers all meals and that no animal-derived dashi or butter is used in the vegan plates when reserving.
places.neighborhood: 鳥羽1丁目（鳥羽国際ホテル）
places.cuisine: リゾートフレンチ・地元海鮮
places.notes: メインダイニング シーホースは鳥羽国際ホテル オーシャンウイングの1階にあり、日本百景にも選ばれた鳥羽湾を眼下に望むリゾートフレンチのメインダイニングです。

chef_bio.background: メインダイニング シーホースは鳥羽国際ホテル オーシャンウイングの1階にあり、日本百景にも選ばれた鳥羽湾を眼下に望むリゾートフレンチのメインダイニングです。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「まさに水揚げされたばかりの旬の味」を、鳥羽の海を望む空間で味わってもらうこと。 サステナブルを意識したコースも用意している。
chef_bio.specialty: 伊勢海老・鮑・松阪牛をいかしたフレンチのガストロノミーコース（ヴォヤージュ／シグネチャー／テロワール／ガストロノミークなどの段階的なコース構成）。

anecdotes.text: 眼下に広がるのは美しい鳥羽の海で、日本百景にも選ばれた景観の中で食事ができる、と公式に紹介されている。
ディナーは伊勢海老のヴォヤージュ、伊勢海老と鮑のシグネチャー、伊勢海老・鮑・松阪牛のガストロノミークなど段階的なコースに加え、ブイヤベースコースとヴィーガンコースを揃えている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: ホテルは、コンタミネーション（混入）等で極微量でも重篤な症状が発症する可能性がある場合、安全を最優先しご依頼をお受けできないことがあると明記している。専用調理ラインの記載はない。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: アレルギー対応では小麦・大豆などを取り扱い対象として挙げているが、フレンチのため小麦は前提的に使用される（パン・ソース等）。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 食物アレルギーがある場合は公式サイトから「食物アレルギーお伺いシート」をダウンロードし、宿泊・予約日の4日前までにFAXまたはメールで送付する運用。
複数の食材にアレルギーがある場合は対応をお受けできないことがある、と明記。

positives.text: フレンチの技法を用いた専用のヴィーガンコースを通常メニューとして提供しており、ヴィーガン宿泊プランも用意されている。
事前申告制ながら卵・乳・小麦・大豆などのアレルギー対応に応じる体制がある。

cultural_comfort.note: A polished resort-hotel dining room used to international guests, with reservation systems in English and formal service, so foreign visitors can expect a comfortable experience.
